meta.title: Mercedes‑Benz S‑Class W221 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.h1: Mercedes‑Benz S‑Class W221 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W221 saloon — from the 3.0‑litre OM642 diesel to the 6.0‑litre M275 V12 in the S 65 AMG. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the S‑Class ownership realities that define this generation. Backed by real UK W221 enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes‑Benz S‑Class W221 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W221 saloon specifically — spanning the pre‑facelift (2005–2009) and facelift (2009–2013) eras.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM642: 90% confidence — well‑documented V6 diesel with oil cooler seal and swirl flap issues. OM629: 85% confidence — robust V8 diesel with 320hp. M272/M273: 85% confidence — proven V6/V8 petrols with balance shaft and timing chain issues. M278: 80% confidence — twin‑turbo V8 successor to M273; generally robust. M157: 85% confidence — AMG twin‑turbo V8 with carbon buildup and oil consumption documented. M275: 85% confidence — V12 biturbo with 517–630hp. All ratings are PROVISIONAL — pending dedicated Engine Pages for each family.

overview.intro: The W221 is the fifth generation of the Mercedes‑Benz S‑Class, produced from August 2005 until June 2013. It succeeded the W220 and was designed as the flagship of the Mercedes range — the "S‑Class among S‑Classes" — with a larger body than its predecessor, significantly more interior space, and a range of V6, V8 and V12 engines. A major 2009 facelift brought the S 400 Hybrid (the first production hybrid from Mercedes), the M278 twin‑turbo V8 replacing the M273, and the OM642 BlueTEC diesel with reduced emissions.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes‑Benz W221 • Predecessor: W220 • Successor: W222 • Sibling body styles in this generation: C216 CL‑Class (coupé).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The S‑Class range (W221/W222 combined) generated real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], with the OM642 diesel and AMG V8s commanding the strongest specialist interest.

items.quote: The proven 3.0‑litre V6 turbo diesel that powered the W221 from 2010 onwards — 258hp, 620Nm of torque, and a documented reputation for longevity. Oil cooler seals are the main watchpoint, but the engine itself is robust and well‑supported across the Mercedes range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want effortless V6 diesel torque with S‑Class refinement.

items.quote: The facelift‑era twin‑turbo V8 delivers 435hp and 700Nm from a 4.7‑litre V8 — smooth, effortless, and a genuine step forward from the M273 it replaced. It's the sensible performance choice in the W221 petrol range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 performance with modern efficiency.

items.quote: The pre‑facelift M272 V6 has a well‑documented balance shaft sprocket wear issue — the sprocket teeth can wear prematurely, causing the timing chain to jump. Early detection is critical; a full rebuild is a four‑figure bill.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who understand the risk and are prepared for potential balance shaft work.

items.quote: The pinnacle of the W221 range — a hand‑built V12 with 630hp and 1000Nm. Coil packs, ignition modules, and cooling system failures are documented on higher‑mileage examples. A full recon is a five‑figure bill, and parts availability is a consideration.
items.whoItsFor: collectors and V12 enthusiasts with deep pockets and a trusted specialist.

items.quote: The pre‑facelift OM642 diesel offers the same robust V6 as the S 350 BlueTEC, but at a lower purchase price. 235hp and 540Nm deliver effortless S‑Class performance, and the engine is proven across the Mercedes range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want S‑Class luxury on a tighter budget.

items.quote: The pre‑facelift S 63 AMG with the M156 6.2‑litre naturally‑aspirated V8 is often overlooked in favour of the facelift M157 twin‑turbo — but the M156 is the last of the great naturally‑aspirated AMG V8s, with 525hp and a soundtrack that no turbocharged engine can match.
items.whoItsFor: AMG enthusiasts who value character over outright pace.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Pre‑Facelift Risk — balance shaft is the defining concern
Proven Diesel — robust but oil cooler‑sensitive

economicsRule.title: The W221 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,200–£5,500 OM642 recon against a £6,000–£15,000 W221 sits at 21–92% of the car's value — on a high‑mileage £6,000 example, that brushes the 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold. Against a well‑kept £12,000+ example, the same recon drops to 27–46% — a routine repair decision, not a financial one. However, a £12,000–£18,000 M275 V12 recon against a £10,000 W221 is 120–180% — on a high‑mileage V12, that's the point where you seriously question whether the car is economically viable. Value the car and its service history first.

problems.title: M272 Balance Shaft Sprocket Wear
problems.rootCause: The balance shaft sprocket on the M272 V6 can wear prematurely — the sprocket teeth are not hardened adequately and can strip, causing the timing chain to jump. A check engine light with camshaft position sensor codes is the classic early warning.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of a check engine light on an M272 S 350, get camshaft position sensor codes read immediately — a £1,000–£2,500 balance shaft repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,000–£5,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M272 engine prices for your W221 →

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Failure
problems.affectedModels: W221 S 320 CDI, S 350 BlueTEC (all OM642.930 / OM642.862).
problems.rootCause: The V6 diesel's oil cooler seals degrade over time, allowing oil to leak into the valley of the V — the labour to access the seals is significant, making this an expensive repair even though the parts are cheap.
problems.recommendation: A £800–£1,800 oil cooler seal replacement is dramatically cheaper than the £3,200–£5,500 replacement that follows oil starvation — address it proactively.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your W221 →

problems.title: M156 Head Bolt & Camshaft Adjuster Failure
problems.rootCause: The M156 6.2‑litre V8 is known for two issues: head bolts can stretch and fail (particularly on early engines), and camshaft adjusters can wear, causing rattle on cold start.
problems.recommendation: On a £15,000–£25,000 S 63 AMG, a £1,500–£3,500 head bolt and camshaft adjuster inspection is cheap insurance — it protects the £7,000–£10,000 engine that sits at the heart of the car.

cta.label: Compare M156 engine prices for your S 63 AMG →

problems.title: W221 AIRMATIC Suspension Wear
problems.affectedModels: All W221 variants, particularly high‑mileage examples.
problems.rootCause: The W221's AIRMATIC air suspension is the single most common non‑engine failure on this platform — air struts can leak, the compressor can fail, and valve block issues are documented.
problems.recommendation: This is the single most common non‑engine W221 failure — have the AIRMATIC system checked on any example over 80,000 miles.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W221 saloon only. C216 CL‑Class shares some of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page.
